mnemonics.title: HONEYPOT Classification
mnemonics.content: L.A.R.R. - Low And Real Research: Low-interaction (emulated services) vs High-interaction (real systems) vs Research honeypots (academic study of attacker behavior)

mnemonics.title: Honeypot Purposes
mnemonics.content: D.E.T.E.R. - Detection, Entertainment, Threat analysis, Evidence collection, Research: The five reasons organizations deploy honeypots

mnemonics.title: Honeynet Components
mnemonics.content: H.I.P.P.O. - Honeypot, Internet, Data control, Data capture, Organization: Key elements that make up a honeynet

mnemonics.title: Detection Methods
mnemonics.content: S.C.A.R.F. - Signature, Anomaly, Rule-based, Fingerprinting: Methods to identify attacks in honeypot environments

acronyms.usage: Monitor single host for suspicious activity; often used alongside honeypots on the same machine
Monitors network traffic for threats; honeypots feed data to NIDS for analysis
Isolated network segment where honeypots are placed to observe attacker activity safely
Place in systems; if accessed, alerts security team - honeypot concept applied to data
Used with honeypots to waste attacker time and resources; delays responses deliberately

visualTips.visualization: Picture a beehive with honey outside - attackers (bears) are attracted to it, but the real hive is hidden elsewhere. The honeypot looks vulnerable but traps/detects the intruder
Think of a spectrum: Low-interaction = cardboard cutout of a person (limited response), High-interaction = a real person who can engage and gather detailed info
Visualize three concentric circles: Inner circle = real production network, Middle circle = honeypots (decoys), Outer circle = internet. Attackers think they're in but are contained
Think of a fishing net with three layers: Layer 1 = packet capture (outer), Layer 2 = system logs (middle), Layer 3 = malware samples (inner) - all feeding into analysis

analogies.analogy: A candy store with a hidden camera in a rough neighborhood - it attracts potential shoplifters, records their behavior, and alerts security without risking real merchandise
A fully furnished model home for sale - looks real and functional, but every room has hidden cameras and sensors to study how visitors behave
A wax museum figure of a bank teller - looks convincing from afar but can't actually process transactions; just tricks someone into trying
A fake expensive watch left visibly unattended in an office - if someone takes it, you know they have sticky fingers and can track them
A museum with one-way mirrors - attackers can enter and move around, but everything they do is watched, recorded, and they can't steal the real artifacts
